title: Validating Docker Container Port Mappings with Port Collision

statement.markdown:
Given a Docker Compose file configuration, validate whether there are any port collisions between services. A port collision occurs when two or more services are assigned to the same port number. You should return the list of services that have port collisions.

Note that a service can have multiple port mappings, but the port number should be the same for a collision to be considered valid.

You can assume that the input is a list of services, where each service is an object with a name and a list of port mappings. The port mappings are represented as objects with a containerPort and a hostPort.

Example:

```json
[{
  "name": "service1",
  "ports": [{ "containerPort": 8080, "hostPort": 8080 }, { "containerPort": 8081, "hostPort": 8081 }]
}, {
  "name": "service2",
  "ports": [{ "containerPort": 8080, "hostPort": 8080 }, { "containerPort": 8082, "hostPort": 8082 }]
}]
```

You should return `['service1']` because service1 has a port collision on port 8080.

You can assume that the input will always be a valid list of services.
statement.inputFormat: A list of services, where each service is an object with a name and a list of port mappings.
statement.outputFormat: A list of service names that have port collisions.
statement.notes: You should use an efficient algorithm to solve this problem. You can use a Set to keep track of the port mappings.

constraints.description: The number of services in the input list.
The number of port mappings for each service.

examples.explanation: Service1 has a port collision on port 8080.
There are no port collisions in this example.
Service1 has a port collision on port 8080.

hints.text: Use a Set to keep track of the port mappings to efficiently detect collisions.
